1: wĕʾîš yiśrāʾēl nišbaʿ bammizpāh lēʾmōr ʾîš mimmennû lōʾ-yittēn bittô lĕbinyāmîn lĕʾiššāh — 'Now the men of Israel had sworn at Mizpah: no man among us will give his daughter to Benjamin in marriage.' The oath (nišbaʿ, H7650, swore — the Niphal) is the first word of ch. 21 but chronologically precedes the assembly in ch. 20. The narrator inserts the oath here to frame the entire resolution crisis: every solution they attempt in ch. 21 is constrained by this vow. The oath structure echoes Jephthah's rash vow (11:30-31) and the oath pattern of Saul in 1 Sam 14:24-45 — hasty assembly oaths that create unforeseen binding constraints.
2: wayyāḇōʾ hāʿām ḇêt-ʾēl wayyēšĕḇû šām ʿad-hāʿerev lipnê hāʾĕlōhîm wayyišʾû qôlām wayyiḇkû ḇĕkî gāḏôl — 'The people came to Bethel and sat before God until evening, lifting their voices and weeping with great weeping.' ḇĕkî gāḏôl (H1065+H1419, great weeping — the superlative construction) — the same phrase used for Jacob's weeping when he reveals himself to Joseph (Gen 45:2) and Joseph's weeping at Jacob's death (Gen 50:1). The communal grief is genuine: they have destroyed their own tribe and now face an irreversible oath. lipnê hāʾĕlōhîm (before God) — the Elohim register again (as in 20:2); the narrator distinguishes levels of divine address.
3: wayyōʾmĕrû lāmmāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hāyĕtāh zōʾt bĕyiśrāʾēl lehippāqēd hayyôm miyyiśrāʾēl šēḇeṭ ʾeḥād — 'They said: O YHWH, God of Israel, why has this happened — that today one tribe should be missing from Israel?' The shift from hāʾĕlōhîm (v.2) to YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl (v.3) in address mirrors ch. 20's progressive approach to the covenant name. lehippāqēd (H6485, that one should be missed/lacking — Niphal: to be numbered, then to be found missing from the count) — the same root used for the wilderness census (Num 1-4); when Israel is numbered, a missing tribe is a covenant gap. The lament form (why, YHWH?) echoes Ps 22:1, Ps 88:14 — the community is modeling the psalmic address of distress.
4: wayyĕhî mimmoḥorāt wayyaškîmû hāʿām wayyiḇnû-šām mizbēăḥ wayyaʿălû ʿōlôt ûšĕlāmîm — 'The next morning the people rose early and built an altar there, offering burnt and peace offerings.' mimmoḥorāt (H4283, on the next day) — the early-morning pattern of devotion (cf. 20:19). The altar-building and dual sacrifice (ʿōlôt ûšĕlāmîm) repeat the full worship of 20:26; the community re-enacts its priestly response before seeking a solution. The altar at Bethel is established without divine command — it is the community's spontaneous act of worship-before-decision, which the text records without censure.
5: wayyōʾmĕrû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl mî ʾăšer lōʾ-ʿālāh baqqāhāl mikkol šibṭê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-YHWH kî hašĕḇûʿāh hağĕdōlāh hāyĕtāh lāʾăšer lōʾ-ʿālāh ʾel-YHWH hammiṣpāh lēʾmōr mwt yûmāt — 'Who of all the tribes of Israel did not come to the assembly before YHWH? For there was a great oath: anyone who did not come up to YHWH at Mizpah shall surely be put to death.' hašĕḇûʿāh hağĕdōlāh (the great oath) — a solemn communal vow with the penalty of death. The oath now creates a second problem: Jabesh-gilead's absence from the assembly makes them oath-violators subject to the death penalty, which then provides the rationale for the ch. 21 massacre — a cascade of covenant mechanisms producing atrocity.
6: wayyinnāḥămû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-binyāmîn ʾāḥîw wayyōʾmĕrû niggĕraʿ hayyôm šēḇeṭ ʾeḥād miyyiśrāʾēl — 'The sons of Israel had compassion for their brother Benjamin and said: one tribe is cut off from Israel today.' wayyinnāḥămû (H5162, had compassion/were consoled — Niphal of nāḥam, the root that means both to comfort and to repent/relent) — the same root used for YHWH's relenting in Exod 32:14 (after the golden calf), Jer 26:19, and throughout the prophets for divine repentance. Here it is Israel's emotional response: they feel regret over what they have done. nāḥam is a deeply ambivalent verb: it can mean grief over a past action, consolation for a present loss, or decision to change course. Israel experiences all three meanings simultaneously.
7: mah-naʿăśeh lāhem lannôtārîm lĕnāšîm waʾănaḥnû nišbaʿnû bĕYHWH lĕḇiltî tēt-lāhem mibbĕnōtênû lĕnāšîm — 'What shall we do for wives for those who remain, since we swore by YHWH that we will not give them our daughters as wives?' The dilemma is stated with rhetorical precision: the two constraints (provide wives; do not break the Mizpah oath) are placed in direct collision. nišbaʿnû bĕYHWH (we swore by YHWH) — invoking the divine name in an oath makes it inviolable (Lev 19:12; Deut 23:21-23: what comes from your lips you must perform). The oath's inviolability is the pressure that generates the Jabesh-gilead and Shiloh solutions.
8: wayyōʾmĕrû mî ʾeḥād miššibṭê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer lōʾ-ʿālāh ʾel-YHWH hammiṣpāh wayhinnēh lōʾ-bāʾ ʾîš ʾel-hammahăneh miyyāḇêš gilʿād ʾel-haqqāhāl — 'They said: who of the tribes of Israel did not come to YHWH at Mizpah? No one from Jabesh-gilead had come.' The discovery of Jabesh-gilead's absence is presented as a rhetorical finding, not a pre-planned maneuver. Jabesh-gilead (H3003, from the east bank of the Jordan) — a city that will later be the site of intense loyalty to Saul (1 Sam 31:11-13: the Jabesh-gileadites retrieve Saul's body from Beth-shan), suggesting a deep historical link between Jabesh-gilead and Benjamin/Saul established partly through this episode.
9: wayyitpāqēd hāʿām wĕhinnēh ʾên-šām ʾîš miyyôšĕḇê yāḇēš gilʿād — 'For when the people were mustered, there was not a man there from the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead.' wayyitpāqēd (H6485, was mustered/numbered) — the census verb completing the forensic inquiry of v.5. The absence is verified by the count, not by accusation. The missing-from-the-count now becomes missing-from-the-land.
10: wayyišlĕḥû šām hāʿēdāh šĕnêm ʿāśār ʾelep ʾîš mibbenê heḥāyil wayyĕṣawwû ʾôtām lēʾmōr lĕkû wahakkîtû ʾet-yôšĕḇê yāḇēš gilʿād leḥepê ḥāreb wĕhannāšîm wĕhattap — 'The congregation sent 12,000 warriors with orders: go and kill the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead with the edge of the sword, including the women and children.' The scale (12,000 men) and the phrasing of the command (leḥepê ḥāreb, by the mouth of the sword) mirror the ḥērem formulas of Joshua. Israel is applying holy-war destruction to an Israelite city — the logical endpoint of the internal fragmentation that began at the opening of the book (1:1).
11: wĕzeh haddābār ʾăšer taʿăśû kol-zākār wĕkol-ʾiššāh yōdaʿat miškab-zākār taḥărîmû — 'This is what you shall do: utterly destroy every male and every woman who has had sexual relations with a man.' taḥărîmû (H2763, devote to destruction — Hiphil of ḥāram, the ḥērem root) — the technical term for the Canaanite destruction command (Josh 6:17; Deut 7:2; 1 Sam 15:3). Applying ḥērem to an Israelite city is unprecedented and signals the total collapse of the covenant boundary between Israel and Canaan — Israel has become its own conquered enemy. yōdaʿat miškab-zākār (who has known the lying of a man — the Mosaic purity formula from Num 31:17-18, applied after Midian) — the criteria for sparing Jabesh-gilead virgins exactly match Moses's command after the Midianite war, another sign of the book's progressive deterioration into its own origins.
12: wayyimṣĕʾû miyyôšĕḇê yāḇēš gilʿād ʾarbāʿ mēʾôt naʿărāh bĕtûlāh ʾăšer lōʾ-yādĕʿāh ʾîš lĕmiškab zākār — 'They found four hundred young virgins among the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead who had not known a man.' bĕtûlāh (H1330, virgin/young woman — the specific term for a woman who has not had sexual relations, distinct from ʿalmāh which emphasizes youth) — the legal precision matters: these women are eligible under the purity criteria. The number 400 is critical: 600 survivors (20:47) minus 400 wives = 200 men still without wives, setting up the second crisis.
13: wayyišlĕḥû kol-hāʿēdāh wayyĕdabbĕrû ʾel-bĕnê binyāmîn ʾăšer bĕselaʿ-hārimôn wayyiqrĕʾû lāhem šālôm — 'The whole congregation sent and called to the Benjamites at the rock of Rimmon in peace.' wayyiqrĕʾû lāhem šālôm (called to them in peace) — the diplomatic overture using šālôm (H7965, peace/wholeness). The same formula appears when Joshua's spies send word to Rahab (Josh 2:21) and in the covenant-making gestures throughout the Hebrew Bible. The community that nearly destroyed Benjamin now extends šālôm — but šālôm without structural repair (the wife problem is still unsolved) is premature.
14: wayyāšob binyāmîn bāʿēt hahîʾ wayyittĕnû lāhem hannāšîm ʾăšer ḥiyyû mibbĕnôt yāḇēš gilʿād wĕlōʾ-māṣĕʾû lāhem kēn — 'Benjamin returned at that time. They were given the women spared from Jabesh-gilead as wives. But there were not enough.' wĕlōʾ-māṣĕʾû lāhem kēn (they did not find enough for them) — the arithmetic gap: 400 women for 600 men. The word kēn (H3651, thus/sufficient) creates the transition: the Jabesh-gilead solution falls short, requiring the Shiloh plan.
15: wĕhāʿām niḥam lĕbinyāmîn kî-ʿāśāh YHWH pereṣ bĕšibṭê yiśrāʾēl — 'The people had compassion for Benjamin, for YHWH had made a gap in the tribes of Israel.' niḥam (H5162, had compassion/relented) — the nāḥam root again (cf. v.6). YHWH ʿāśāh pereṣ (YHWH made a gap/breach — H6556, pereṣ from pāraṣ, to break through) — the divine agency is acknowledged: the gap in the tribes is attributed to YHWH. This is not a distancing move but a theological affirmation: the outcome of the war, including the near-extinction of Benjamin, was within YHWH's providential purpose. The same root pāraṣ is used for YHWH breaking through at Baal-perazim (2 Sam 5:20: YHWH pāraṣ ʿōyĕḇay) and for the infant's breakthrough at birth (Gen 38:29: mah-pāraṣtā ʿālêkā pāreṣ, hence the name Perez).
16: wayyōʾmĕrû ziqĕnê hāʿēdāh mah naʿăśeh lannôtārîm lĕnāšîm kî-nišmĕdāh mibinyāmîn ʾiššāh — 'The elders of the congregation said: what shall we do for wives for those who are left, since the women of Benjamin have been destroyed?' The elders (ziqĕnê hāʿēdāh, H2205+H5712, elders of the congregation) — the formal leadership structure. nišmĕdāh (H8045, has been destroyed/annihilated — Niphal of šāmad, used for the destruction of nations in Deut 9:3; Josh 7:12) — the verb for the total destruction of enemies is applied to Israelite women. The elders acknowledge the consequence of their own military action.
17: wayyōʾmĕrû yĕruššat pĕlêṭāh lĕbinyāmîn wĕlōʾ-yimmāḥeh šēḇeṭ miyyiśrāʾēl — 'There must be an inheritance for the escaped remnant of Benjamin, so that a tribe may not be wiped out from Israel.' yĕruššāh (H3425, inheritance/possession — from yāraš, to inherit/possess) — the covenant-inheritance term; Benjamin must have its yĕruššāh to maintain its place in the covenant structure. pĕlêṭāh (H6413, escaped remnant/survivors — from pālaṭ, to escape) — the term for the surviving remnant of judgment (Isa 10:20; Ezra 9:8, 13). The language of remnant-preservation for Benjamin anticipates the theological vocabulary of the prophets: even in judgment, a pĕlêṭāh survives, because the covenant purposes require it.
18: waʾănaḥnû lōʾ nûkal lātēt lāhem nāšîm mibběnōtênû kî-nišbĕʿû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr ʾārûr nōtēn ʾiššāh lĕbinyāmîn — 'But we cannot give them wives from our daughters, for the sons of Israel swore: cursed is anyone who gives a wife to Benjamin.' ʾārûr (H779, cursed — the Qal passive participle of ʾārar, used in the covenant curses of Deut 27-28, e.g., Deut 27:15-26: twelve curse-formulas beginning with ʾārûr) — the oath is not just a social agreement but a covenant-curse invocation. Breaking it would bring the Deuteronomic curse upon the oath-breaker. The community has locked itself inside a self-constructed curse-framework.
19: wayyōʾmĕrû hinnēh ḥag-YHWH bĕšîlōh miyyāmîm yāmîmāh ʾăšer mişṣĕpônāh lĕḇêt-ʾēl mizrĕḥāh haššemšāh ûminnegeḇ lĕ-lĕḇônāh — 'They said: there is a yearly festival of YHWH at Shiloh, north of Bethel, east of the road from Bethel to Shechem, and south of Lebonah.' ḥag-YHWH (H2282+H3068, feast of YHWH) — the technical term for the three major pilgrimage feasts (Exod 23:14-17: Unleavened Bread, Harvest/Weeks, Ingathering/Booths). The feast at Shiloh is almost certainly Sukkot (Booths/Tabernacles), associated with dancing and celebration (Ps 149:3; Ps 150:4). The precise geographical directions given for Shiloh (north of Bethel, east of the road) have the character of official geographic notation — the narrator is being precise because Shiloh is the legitimate sanctuary site (Josh 18:1; 1 Sam 1:3).
20: wayyĕṣawwû ʾet-bĕnê binyāmîn lēʾmōr lĕkû waʾăraḇtem bakkeramîm — 'They commanded the Benjamites: go and lie in wait in the vineyards.' waʾăraḇtem (H693, lie in wait/ambush — the same ʾāraḇ root used for Israel's ambush in 20:29) — the ambush vocabulary from the civil war is now repurposed for abduction at a sacred festival. The strategic counsel is given by the elders to enable what the narrative presents as a legal workaround: they technically do not give their daughters, so the oath is not violated, but they facilitate the seizure. The moral logic is casuistry of the worst kind.
21: ûrĕʾîtem wĕhinnēh ʾim-yēṣĕʾû bĕnôt šîlōh lĕḥwl bamḥōlôt ûṣĕʾtem min-hakkeramîm waḥtaptem lākem ʾîš ʾiššĕtô mibbĕnôt šîlōh wahalaktem ʾereṣ binyāmîn — 'Watch, and when the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in the dances, come out of the vineyards — each man seize his wife from the daughters of Shiloh and go to Benjamin.' waḥtaptem (H2414, seize/snatch — ḥāṭap, a verb used rarely; elsewhere for predatory seizing) — a violent abduction verb. lĕḥwl bamḥōlôt (to dance in the dances — ḥûl/ḥôl in the dance circles, used in Ps 149:3 and Jer 31:13 for communal celebration dance) — the daughters of Shiloh are seized from a sacred festival, a violation compounded by its sacred setting. ḥāṭap does not appear in Deut 24 (laws on marriage), signaling this is outside normal covenantal procedure.
22: wĕhāyāh kî-yāḇōʾû ʾăḇôtām ʾô ʾăḥêhem lārîḇ ʾēlênû wĕʾāmarnû ʾălêhem ḥānnûnû ʾôtām kî lōʾ lāqaḥnû ʾîš ʾiššetô bammilḥāmāh kî-lōʾ ʾattem nĕtatttem lāhem kĕʿēt tiʾšāmû — 'When their fathers or brothers come to us to complain, we will say: grant them to us as a gracious act, because we did not take each man a wife in battle; you did not give them, so you are not guilty.' ḥānnûnû ʾôtām (be gracious to them — Qal imperative of ḥānan, the root of grace/favor, cf. Ps 123:3: ḥānnênû YHWH ḥānnênû) — the appeal for grace toward Benjamin uses the vocabulary of prayer addressed to YHWH. The legal argument (you did not give, therefore you are not oath-bound) is technically correct but morally hollow: the elders are using a legal loophole to rationalize mass abduction.
23: wayyaʿăśû kēn bĕnê binyāmîn wayyiśśĕʾû nāšîm lĕmispārām min-hammĕḥōlĕlôt ʾăšer gāzālû wayyēlĕkû wayyāšûḇû ʾel-naḥălātām wayyiḇnû ʾet-hĕʿārîm wayyēšĕḇû bāhem — 'The Benjamites did so. They seized wives for each of them from the dancers, went back to their territory, rebuilt the cities, and lived in them.' gāzālû (H1497, they seized/stole — gāzal, to tear away, the same root as Micah's idols stolen in 18:24 and the vocabulary of robbery in Lev 19:13; Prov 22:22) — the narrator uses the robbery verb rather than the marriage verb. The rebuilding of the cities (wayyiḇnû ʾet-hĕʿārîm) ends the account on a note of restoration, but without divine endorsement or covenant ceremony. Benjamin reconstitutes itself by theft from a sacred festival.
24: wayyēlĕkû miššām bĕnê yiśrāʾēl bāʿēt hahîʾ ʾîš lĕšibṭô ûlĕmišpaḥtô wayyēṣĕʾû miššām ʾîš lĕnaḥălātô — 'At that time the sons of Israel departed, each to his tribe and family, each going out to his inheritance.' ʾîš lĕnaḥălātô (each to his inheritance) — the verse ends with the covenant-inheritance term, mirroring the distribution language of Joshua 13-21. The tribes return to their allotted territories: the covenant structure formally reconstitutes itself, but the method of Benjamin's reconstitution (massacre + abduction) lies beneath this ordered dispersal.
25: bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên melek bĕyiśrāʾēl ʾîš hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw yaʿăśeh — 'In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.' The book's closing refrain and final verdict. ʾên melek bĕyiśrāʾēl (no king in Israel) — the positive statement of the book's central theological diagnosis. Judges was written retrospectively from the monarchy period; the refrain functions as both explanation (why all this chaos happened) and anticipation (what was needed). hayyāšār bĕʿênāyw (what was right in his own eyes — H3477+H5869, rightness in his eyes) — contrasted with Deuteronomy's standard: do what is right in the eyes of YHWH (hayyāšār bĕʿênê YHWH, Deut 12:25; 13:18; 21:9). The substitution of ʿênāyw (his own eyes) for ʿênê YHWH (eyes of YHWH) is the anatomy of the book's failure: private judgment substituted for covenant law. The closing verse of Judges is the OT's most compressed statement of the problem that the messianic hope answers — the need for a king who embodies the will of YHWH and whose rule aligns human action with divine righteousness.
